Sys. 1989 | | [GCJ-02](/wiki/Restrictions_on_geographic_data_in_China "Restrictions on geographic data in China") | Chinese obfuscated datum 2002 | | [Geo URI](/wiki/Geo_URI_scheme "Geo URI scheme") | Internet link to a point 2010 |  * [International Terrestrial Reference System](/wiki/International_Terrestrial_Reference_System "International Terrestrial Reference System") * [Spatial Reference System Identifier (SRID)](/wiki/SRID "SRID") * [Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM)](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator_coordinate_system "Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system")

(**BDS**; [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 北斗卫星导航系统; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *běidǒu wèixīng dǎoháng xìtǒng*) is a [satellite-based radio navigation system](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") owned and operated by the [China National Space Administration](/wiki/China_National_Space_Administration "China National Space Administration").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It provides geolocation and time information to a BDS receiver anywhere on or near the Earth where there is an unobstructed line of sight to four or more BDS satellites.[[5]](#cite_note-5) It does not require the user to transmit any data and operates independently of any telephonic or Internet reception, though these technologies can enhance the usefulness of the BDS positioning information.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

(third-generation BeiDou), provides full global coverage for timing and navigation, along with Russia's [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS"), the European [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), and the US's [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It comprises satellites in three types of orbits: 24 in medium Earth orbit (global coverage), 3 in inclined geosynchronous orbit (Asia–Pacific coverage), and 3 in geostationary orbit (China coverage). The BeiDou-3 system was fully operational in July 2020.[[8]](#cite_note-chinanews_20150331-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2016, BeiDou-3 reached millimeter-level accuracy with post-processing from a nationwide reference station network.[[13]](#cite_note-beidou-road-to-global-service-13)

(first-generation BeiDou), consisting of three satellites in a [regional satellite navigation system](/wiki/Regional_satellite_navigation_system "Regional satellite navigation system"). Since 2000, the system has mainly provided navigation services within China. In December 2012, as the design life of BeiDou-1 expired, it stopped operating.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

(second-generation BeiDou) system was also a regional satellite navigation system containing 16 satellites, including 6 geostationary satellites, 6 inclined geosynchronous orbit satellites, and 4 medium earth orbit satellites. In November 2012, BeiDou-2 began to provide users with regional positioning services in the [Asia-Pacific region](/wiki/Asia%E2%80%93Pacific "Asia–Pacific").[[15]](#cite_note-op_china-15)[[16]](#cite_note-CustomersDec2012-16) Within the region, BeiDou is more accurate than GPS.[[17]](#cite_note-:03-17): 179

The **BeiDou Navigation Satellite System** (**BDS**; [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 北斗卫星导航系统; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *běidǒu wèixīng dǎoháng xìtǒng*) is a [satellite-based radio navigation system](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") owned and operated by the [China National Space Administration](/wiki/China_National_Space_Administration "China National Space Administration").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It provides geolocation and time information to a BDS receiver anywhere on or near the Earth where there is an unobstructed line of sight to four or more BDS satellites.[[5]](#cite_note-5) It does not require the user to transmit any data and operates independently of any telephonic or Internet reception, though these technologies can enhance the usefulness of the BDS positioning information.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The current service, **BeiDou-3** (third-generation BeiDou), provides full global coverage for timing and navigation, along with Russia's [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS"), the European [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), and the US's [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It comprises satellites in three types of orbits: 24 in medium Earth orbit (global coverage), 3 in inclined geosynchronous orbit (Asia–Pacific coverage), and 3 in geostationary orbit (China coverage). The BeiDou-3 system was fully operational in July 2020.[[8]](#cite_note-chinanews_20150331-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2016, BeiDou-3 reached millimeter-level accuracy with post-processing from a nationwide reference station network.[[13]](#cite_note-beidou-road-to-global-service-13)

Predecessors included **BeiDou-1** (first-generation BeiDou), consisting of three satellites in a [regional satellite navigation system](/wiki/Regional_satellite_navigation_system "Regional satellite navigation system"). Since 2000, the system has mainly provided navigation services within China. In December 2012, as the design life of BeiDou-1 expired, it stopped operating.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
The **BeiDou-2** (second-generation BeiDou) system was also a regional satellite navigation system containing 16 satellites, including 6 geostationary satellites, 6 inclined geosynchronous orbit satellites, and 4 medium earth orbit satellites. In November 2012, BeiDou-2 began to provide users with regional positioning services in the [Asia-Pacific region](/wiki/Asia%E2%80%93Pacific "Asia–Pacific").[[15]](#cite_note-op_china-15)[[16]](#cite_note-CustomersDec2012-16) Within the region, BeiDou is more accurate than GPS.[[17]](#cite_note-:03-17): 179

In 2015, fifteen years after the satellite system was launched, it was generating a turnover of $31.5 billion per annum for major companies such as [China Aerospace Science and Industry Corporation](/wiki/China_Aerospace_Science_and_Industry_Corporation "China Aerospace Science and Industry Corporation"), [AutoNavi](/wiki/AutoNavi "AutoNavi"), and [Norinco](/wiki/Norinco "Norinco").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The industry has grown an average of over 20% in value annually to reach $64 billion in 2020.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2023, the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") recognized the BeiDou system as a global standard for commercial aviation.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

The official English name of the system is *BeiDou Navigation Satellite System*.[[22]](#cite_note-22) It is named after the [Big Dipper](/wiki/Big_Dipper "Big Dipper") [asterism](/wiki/Asterism_(astronomy) "Asterism (astronomy)"), which is known in Chinese as *Běidǒu* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 北斗). The name literally means "Northern Dipper", the name given by ancient Chinese astronomers to the seven brightest stars of the [Ursa Major constellation](/wiki/Ursa_Major_constellation "Ursa Major constellation").[[23]](#cite_note-iTWire-23) Historically, this set of stars was used in navigation to locate the [North Star](/wiki/Polaris "Polaris"). As such, the name BeiDou also serves as a metaphor for the purpose of the satellite navigation system.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Conception and initial development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Conception and initial development")]

The original idea of a Chinese satellite navigation system was conceived by [Chen Fangyun](/wiki/Chen_Fangyun "Chen Fangyun") and his colleagues in the 1980s.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") in 1991 showcased how the GPS gave the US complete advantage on the battlefield and how satellite navigation systems can be used to conduct "space warfare".[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 1993, China realised the risk of denied access to GPS during the [Yinhe incident](/wiki/Yinhe_incident "Yinhe incident") and including an alleged case in 1996 during the [Third Taiwan Strait Crisis](/wiki/Third_Taiwan_Strait_Crisis "Third Taiwan Strait Crisis"), gave impetus to the creation of its own indigenous satellite navigation system which officially began in 1994.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

According to the [China National Space Administration](/wiki/China_National_Space_Administration "China National Space Administration"), in 2010, the development of the system would be carried out in three steps:[[27]](#cite_note-CNSA_2010-27)

1. 2000–2003: experimental BeiDou navigation system consisting of three satellites
2. By 2012: regional BeiDou navigation system covering China and neighboring regions
3. By 2020: global BeiDou navigation system

The first satellite, *BeiDou-1A*, was launched on 30 October 2000, followed by *BeiDou-1B* on 20 December 2000. The third satellite, *BeiDou-1C* (a backup satellite), was put into orbit on 25 May 2003.[[28]](#cite_note-CNSA_2003-28) The successful launch of *BeiDou-1C* also meant the establishment of the BeiDou-1 navigation system.

On 2 November 2006, China announced that from 2008 BeiDou would offer an open service with an accuracy of 10 metres, timing of 0.2 microseconds, and speed of 0.2 metres/second.[[29]](#cite_note-NewScientist-29)

In February 2007, the fourth and last satellite of the BeiDou-1 system, *BeiDou-1D* (sometimes called *BeiDou-2A*, serving as a backup satellite), was launched.[[30]](#cite_note-gov.cn-30) It was reported that the satellite had suffered from a control system malfunction but was then fully restored.[[31]](#cite_note-SinoDefence-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In April 2007, the first satellite of BeiDou-2, namely *Compass-M1* (to validate frequencies for the BeiDou-2 constellation) was successfully put into its working orbit. The second BeiDou-2 constellation satellite *Compass-G2* was launched on 15 April 2009.[[33]](#cite_note-ASM-33)

On 15 January 2010, the official website of the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System went online,[[34]](#cite_note-Compass-G1-34) and the system's third satellite (*Compass-G1*) was carried into its orbit by a [Long March 3C](/wiki/Long_March_3C "Long March 3C") rocket on 17 January 2010.[[34]](#cite_note-Compass-G1-34)

On 2 June 2010, the fourth satellite was launched successfully into orbit.[[35]](#cite_note-SpaceflightNow-35)

The fifth orbiter was launched into space from [Xichang Satellite Launch Center](/wiki/Xichang_Satellite_Launch_Center "Xichang Satellite Launch Center") by an [LM-3I](/wiki/Long_March_3 "Long March 3") carrier rocket on 1 August 2010.[[36]](#cite_note-Xinhua0810-36)

Three months later, on 1 November 2010, the sixth satellite was sent into orbit by LM-3C.[[37]](#cite_note-cngov1101-37)

Another satellite, the BeiDou-2/Compass IGSO-5 (fifth inclined geosynchronous orbit) satellite, was launched from the Xichang Satellite Launch Center by a [Long March 3A](/wiki/Long_March_3A "Long March 3A") on 1 December 2011 (UTC).[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Chinese involvement in Galileo system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Chinese involvement in Galileo system")]

In September 2003, China intended to join the European [Galileo positioning system](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)") project and was to invest [€](/wiki/Euro "Euro")230 million ([US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")296 million, [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")160 million) in Galileo over the next few years.[[39]](#cite_note-39) At the time, it was believed that China's "BeiDou" navigation system would then only be used by its armed forces.[[29]](#cite_note-NewScientist-29)

In October 2004, China officially joined the Galileo project by signing the *Agreement on the Cooperation in the Galileo Program between the "Galileo Joint Undertaking" (GJU) and the "National Remote Sensing Centre of China" (NRSCC)*.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Based on the Sino-European Cooperation Agreement on Galileo program, China Galileo Industries (CGI),[[41]](#cite_note-41) the prime contractor of China's involvement in Galileo programs, was founded in December 2004.[[42]](#cite_note-42) By April 2006, eleven cooperation projects within the Galileo framework had been signed between China and the EU.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Phase III")]

Model of BeiDou Phase III satellite orbits

* In November 2014, BeiDou became part of the World-Wide Radionavigation System (WWRNS) at the 94th meeting of the [International Maritime Organization](/wiki/International_Maritime_Organization "International Maritime Organization") (IMO) Maritime Safety Committee,[[44]](#cite_note-44) which approved the "Navigation Safety Circular" of the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System (BDS).[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* At Beijing time 21:52, 30 March 2015, the first new-generation BeiDou Navigation satellite (and the 17th overall) was successfully set to orbit by a [Long March 3C](/wiki/Long_March_3C "Long March 3C") rocket.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-BeiDou-launch-17-48)
* On 20 April 2019, a BeiDou satellite was successfully launched. Launch occurred at 22:41 [Beijing time](/wiki/Beijing_time "Beijing time"), and the Long March 3B delivered the BeiDou navigation payload into an elliptical transfer orbit ranging between 220 kilometres and 35,787 kilometres, with an inclination of 28.5° to the equator, according to U.S. military tracking data.[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* On 23 June 2020, the final BeiDou satellite was successfully launched, the launch of the 55th satellite in the BeiDou family. The third iteration of the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System provides global coverage for timing and navigation, offering an alternative to Russia's [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS") and the European [Galileo positioning system](/wiki/Galileo_(satellite_navigation) "Galileo (satellite navigation)"), as well as the US's [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Use outside China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Use outside China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp/250px-GPSTest_screenshot_%282025%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:GPSTest_screenshot_(2025).webp)

Screenshot of GPSTest application showing BeiDou satellites usage in [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") (2025)

In 2018, the [Pakistan Armed Forces](/wiki/Pakistan_Armed_Forces "Pakistan Armed Forces") received access to BeiDou for military purposes.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51) In 2019, the Saudi [Ministry of Defense](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Saudi_Arabia) "Ministry of Defense (Saudi Arabia)") signed an agreement for military use of BeiDou.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51) In 2020, Argentina entered into a cooperation agreement with China regarding the use of BeiDou.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-52) In 2021, the first China-Africa BeiDou System Cooperation Forum was held in Beijing.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-52) In 2022, Russian President [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") signed an agreement for the interoperability of BeiDou and [GLONASS](/wiki/GLONASS "GLONASS").[[51]](#cite_note-:1-51)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Use in smartphones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Use in smartphones")]

Chinese smartphone maker [Xiaomi](/wiki/Xiaomi "Xiaomi") featured BeiDou as early as August 2014, on their [Mi 4](/wiki/Xiaomi_Mi_4 "Xiaomi Mi 4").[[54]](#cite_note-54) The earliest known smartphone by South-Korean smartphone maker Samsung to feature BeiDou is the [Samsung Galaxy Note 4](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_4 "Samsung Galaxy Note 4"), released in October 2014.[[55]](#cite_note-55) At a similar time, Japanese phone maker Sony implemented BeiDou for the first time on their Xperia Z3.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The earliest BeiDou-capable smartphone by American smartphone maker Apple is the [iPhone 13](/wiki/IPhone_13 "IPhone 13"), released in September 2021.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## GPS vs. BeiDou Capabilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: GPS vs. BeiDou Capabilities")]

The National Space-Based Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) Advisory Board, which offers independent guidance to the U.S. government on GPS policy, issued a summary report from its 27th meeting held on 16–17 November 2022. During the meeting, it was highlighted that "GPS’s capabilities are now substantially inferior to those of China’s BeiDou."[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## BeiDou-3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: BeiDou-3")]

[![Hydrogen maser used by Beidou-3.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Beidou_atomic_clock_at_CSTM_02.jpg/250px-Beidou_atomic_clock_at_CSTM_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beidou_atomic_clock_at_CSTM_02.jpg)

[Hydrogen maser](/wiki/Hydrogen_maser "Hydrogen maser") used by BeiDou-3

The third phase of the BeiDou system (BDS-3) includes three GEO satellites, three IGSO satellites, and twenty-four MEO satellites which introduce new signal frequencies B1C/B1I/B1A (1575.42 MHz), B2a/B2b (1191.79 MHz), B3I/B3Q/B3A (1268.52 MHz), and Bs test frequency (2492.02 MHz). Interface control documents on the new open signals were published in 2017–2018.[[59]](#cite_note-auto-59)

On 23 June 2020, the BDS-3 constellation deployment was fully completed after the last satellite was successfully launched at the [Xichang Satellite Launch Center](/wiki/Xichang_Satellite_Launch_Center "Xichang Satellite Launch Center").[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) BDS-3 satellites also include [SBAS](/wiki/Satellite-based_augmentation_system "Satellite-based augmentation system") (B1C, B2a, B1A - GEO sats), [Precise Point Positioning](/wiki/Precise_Point_Positioning "Precise Point Positioning") (B2b - GEO sats),[[62]](#cite_note-62) and [search and rescue transponder](/wiki/Search_and_rescue_transponder "Search and rescue transponder") (6 [MEOSAR](/wiki/MEOSAR "MEOSAR")) capabilities.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Predecessors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Predecessors")]

### BeiDou-1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: BeiDou-1")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Beidou-coverage.png/250px-Beidou-coverage.png)](/wiki/File:Beidou-coverage.png)

Coverage polygon of BeiDou-1

BeiDou-1 was an experimental regional navigation system, which consisted of four satellites (three working satellites and one backup satellite). The satellites themselves were based on the Chinese [DFH-3](/wiki/Dong_Fang_Hong "Dong Fang Hong") geostationary communications satellite and had a launch weight of 1,000 kg each.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Unlike the American [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"), Russian GLONASS, and European [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_positioning_system "Galileo positioning system") systems, which use [medium Earth orbit](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") satellites, BeiDou-1 used satellites in [geostationary orbit](/wiki/Geostationary_orbit "Geostationary orbit"). This means that the system does not require a large constellation of satellites, but it also limits the coverage to areas on Earth where the satellites are visible.[[28]](#cite_note-CNSA_2003-28) The area that can be serviced is from longitude 70° E to 140° E and from latitude 5° N to 55° N. The frequency of the system is 2,491.75 MHz.[[31]](#cite_note-SinoDefence-31)

#### Completion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Completion")]

The first satellite, BeiDou-1A, was launched on 31 October 2000. The second satellite, BeiDou-1B, was successfully launched on 21 December 2000. The last operational satellite of the constellation, BeiDou-1C, was launched on 25 May 2003.[[28]](#cite_note-CNSA_2003-28)

#### Position calculation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Position calculation")]

In 2007, the official [Xinhua News Agency](/wiki/Xinhua_News_Agency "Xinhua News Agency") reported that the resolution of the BeiDou system was as high as 0.5 metre.[[68]](#cite_note-68) With the existing user terminals it appears that the calibrated accuracy is 20 m (100 m, uncalibrated).[[69]](#cite_note-69)

#### Terminals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Terminals")]

In 2008, a BeiDou-1 ground terminal cost around [CN¥](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") 20,000 ([US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")2,929), almost 10 times the price of a contemporary GPS terminal.[[70]](#cite_note-70) The price of the terminals was explained as being due to the cost of imported microchips.[[71]](#cite_note-71) At the China High-Tech Fair ELEXCON of November 2009 in [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen"), a BeiDou terminal priced at [CN¥](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") 3,000 was presented.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

#### Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Applications")]

* Over 1000 BeiDou-1 terminals were used after the [2008 Sichuan earthquake](/wiki/2008_Sichuan_earthquake "2008 Sichuan earthquake"), providing information from the disaster area.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* As of October 2009, all Chinese border guards in [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") were equipped with BeiDou-1 devices.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

[Sun Jiadong](/wiki/Sun_Jiadong "Sun Jiadong"), the chief designer of the navigation system, said in 2010 that "Many organizations have been using our system for a while, and they like it very much".[[75]](#cite_note-75)

#### Decommissioning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Decommissioning")]

BeiDou-1 was decommissioned at the end of 2012, after the BeiDou-2 system became operational.

### BeiDou-2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: BeiDou-2")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Beidou_Navigation_Satellite_System_2012.png/250px-Beidou_Navigation_Satellite_System_2012.png)](/wiki/File:Beidou_Navigation_Satellite_System_2012.png)

Coverage polygon of BeiDou-2 in 2012

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Gps_compass_galileo_frequency_allocation_Asimsky_05_2008.jpg/330px-Gps_compass_galileo_frequency_allocation_Asimsky_05_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gps_compass_galileo_frequency_allocation_Asimsky_05_2008.jpg)

Frequency allocation of [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"), [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_positioning_system "Galileo positioning system"), and COMPASS; the light red color of E1 band indicates that the transmission in this band has not yet been detected.

BeiDou-2 (formerly known as COMPASS)[[76]](#cite_note-76) is not an extension to the older BeiDou-1, but rather supersedes it outright. The new system is a constellation of 35 satellites, which include 5 geostationary orbit satellites for backward compatibility with BeiDou-1, and 30 non-geostationary satellites (27 in [medium Earth orbit](/wiki/Medium_Earth_orbit "Medium Earth orbit") and 3 in inclined [geosynchronous orbit](/wiki/Geosynchronous_orbit "Geosynchronous orbit")),[[77]](#cite_note-InsideGNSS-77) that offer complete coverage of the globe.

The ranging signals are based on the [CDMA](/wiki/CDMA "CDMA") principle and have complex structure typical of [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo_positioning_system "Galileo positioning system") or modernized [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS"). Similar to the other [global navigation satellite systems](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") (GNSSs), there are two levels of positioning service: open (public) and restricted (military). The public service is available globally to general users. When all the currently planned GNSSs are deployed, users of multi-constellation receivers will benefit from a total over 100 satellites, which will significantly improve all aspects of positioning, especially availability of the signals in so-called [urban canyons](/wiki/Urban_canyons "Urban canyons").[[78]](#cite_note-78) The general designer of the COMPASS navigation system is [Sun Jiadong](/wiki/Sun_Jiadong "Sun Jiadong"), who is also the general designer of its predecessor, the original BeiDou navigation system.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] All BeiDou satellites are equipped with laser [retroreflector](/wiki/Retroreflector "Retroreflector") arrays for [satellite laser ranging](/wiki/Satellite_laser_ranging "Satellite laser ranging")[[79]](#cite_note-Quality_assessment_of_experimental-79) and the verification of the orbit quality.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

#### Accuracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Accuracy")]

There are two levels of service provided, a free service to civilians and licensed service to the [Chinese government](/wiki/Chinese_government "Chinese government") and military.[[35]](#cite_note-SpaceflightNow-35)[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) The free civilian service has a 10-metre location-tracking accuracy, synchronizes clocks with an accuracy of 10 nanoseconds, and measures speeds to within 0.2 m/s. The restricted military service has a location accuracy of 10 cm,[[84]](#cite_note-84) can be used for communication, and will supply information about the system status to the user.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2019, the International GNSS Service started providing precise orbits of BeiDou satellites in experimental products.[[79]](#cite_note-Quality_assessment_of_experimental-79)

To date, the military service has been granted only to the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") and to the [Pakistan Armed Forces](/wiki/Pakistan_Armed_Forces "Pakistan Armed Forces").[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

#### Frequencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Frequencies")]

Frequencies for COMPASS are allocated in four bands: E1, E2, E5B, and E6; they overlap with Galileo. The fact of overlapping could be convenient from the point of view of the receiver design, but on the other hand raises the issues of system interference, especially within E1 and E2 bands, which are allocated for Galileo's publicly regulated service.[[88]](#cite_note-88) However, under [International Telecommunication Union](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union") (ITU) policies, the first nation to start broadcasting in a specific frequency will have priority to that frequency, and any subsequent users will be required to obtain permission prior to using that frequency, and otherwise ensure that their broadcasts do not interfere with the original nation's broadcasts. As of 2009, it appeared that Chinese COMPASS satellites would start transmitting in the E1, E2, E5B, and E6 bands before Europe's Galileo satellites and thus have primary rights to these frequency ranges.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

#### Compass-M1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Compass-M1")]

Compass-M1 is an experimental satellite launched for signal testing and validation and for the frequency filing on 14 April 2007. The role of Compass-M1 for Compass is similar to the role of the [GIOVE](/wiki/GIOVE "GIOVE") satellites for the Galileo system. The orbit of Compass-M1 is nearly circular, has an altitude of 21,150 km and an inclination of 55.5°.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The investigation of the transmitted signals started immediately after the launch of Compass-M1 on 14 April 2007. Soon after in June 2007, engineers at [CNES](/wiki/CNES "CNES") reported the spectrum and structure of the signals.[[90]](#cite_note-90) A month later, researchers from [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") reported the complete decoding of the "I" signals components.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92) The knowledge of the codes allowed a group of engineers at [Septentrio](/wiki/Septentrio "Septentrio") to build the COMPASS receiver[[93]](#cite_note-93) and report tracking and multipath characteristics of the "I" signals on E2 and E5B.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

#### Operation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Operation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Ground_track_beidou-m2_satellite.jpg/250px-Ground_track_beidou-m2_satellite.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ground_track_beidou-m2_satellite.jpg)

[Ground track](/wiki/Ground_track "Ground track") of BeiDou-M5 (2012-050A)

In December 2011, the system went into operation on a trial basis.[[95]](#cite_note-CD69-95) It started providing navigation, positioning and timing data to China and the neighbouring area for free from 27 December 2011. During this trial run, Compass offered positioning accuracy to within 25 metres and the precision improved as more satellites were launched. Upon the system's official launch, it pledged to offer general users positioning information accurate to the nearest 10 m, measure speeds within 0.2 metres per second, and provide signals for clock synchronisation accurate to 0.02 microseconds.[[96]](#cite_note-BBC8-96)

The BeiDou-2 system began offering services for the Asia-Pacific region in December 2012.[[16]](#cite_note-CustomersDec2012-16) At this time, the system could provide positioning data between longitude 55° E to 180° E and from latitude 55° S to 55° N.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

The new-generation BeiDou satellites support short message service.[[48]](#cite_note-BeiDou-launch-17-48)

#### Completion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Completion")]

In December 2011, [Xinhua](/wiki/Xinhua "Xinhua") stated that "[t]he basic structure of the BeiDou system has now been established, and engineers are now conducting comprehensive system test and evaluation. The system will provide test-run services of positioning, navigation and time for China and the neighboring areas before the end of this year, according to the authorities".[[98]](#cite_note-98) The system became operational in the China region that same month.[[15]](#cite_note-op_china-15) The global navigation system should be finished by 2020.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

As of December 2012, 16 satellites for BeiDou-2 had been launched, with 14 in service. As of December 2017, 150 million Chinese smartphones (20% of the market) were equipped to utilize BeiDou.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Main article: [List of BeiDou satellites](/wiki/List_of_BeiDou_satellites "List of BeiDou satellites")

The regional BeiDou-1 system was decommissioned at the end of 2012.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first satellite of the second-generation system, Compass-M1 was launched in 2007. It was followed by further nine satellites during 2009–2011, achieving functional regional coverage. A total of 16 satellites were launched during this phase.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2015, the system began its transition towards global coverage with the first launch of a new-generation of satellites,[[48]](#cite_note-BeiDou-launch-17-48) and the 17th one within the new system. On 25 July 2015, the 18th and 19th satellites were successfully launched from the [Xichang Satellite Launch Center](/wiki/Xichang_Satellite_Launch_Center "Xichang Satellite Launch Center"), marking the first time for China to launch two satellites at once on top of a [Long March 3B](/wiki/Long_March_3B "Long March 3B")/[Yuanzheng-1](/wiki/Yuanzheng-1 "Yuanzheng-1") [carrier rocket](/wiki/Launch_vehicle "Launch vehicle"). The Yuanzheng-1 is an independent [upper stage](/wiki/Multistage_rocket "Multistage rocket") capable of delivering one or more spacecraft into different orbits. On 29 September 2015, the 20th satellite was launched, carrying a [hydrogen maser](/wiki/Hydrogen_maser "Hydrogen maser") for the first time within the system.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

In 2016, the 21st, 22nd and 23rd satellites were launched from [Xichang Satellite Launch Center](/wiki/Xichang_Satellite_Launch_Center "Xichang Satellite Launch Center"),[[102]](#cite_note-102) the last two of which entered into service on 5 August and 30 November, respectively.[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)

Orbital period: 12 hours and 53 minutes (every 13 revolutions, done in 7 sidereal days, [a satellite passes over the same location](/wiki/Satellite_revisit_period "Satellite revisit period")).[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Concerns and prohibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=BeiDou&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Concerns and prohibitions")]

Concerns have been raised by Taiwan's [Ministry of Science and Technology](/wiki/National_Science_and_Technology_Council_(Taiwan) "National Science and Technology Council (Taiwan)") about the possibility of embedded malware in BeiDou-enabled hardware and software.[[106]](#cite_note-106) In 2018, Taiwan's [National Communications Commission](/wiki/National_Communications_Commission "National Communications Commission") announced that it would be illegal to use BeiDou products in Taiwan without its approval.[[107]](#cite_note-107)
